Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings to listen to recorded lectures and go through materials, but life kept getting in the way. I'd plan to study for a few hours each day, but I ended up cramming most of my work into longer sessions on weekends. I learn best by hearing things, so I'd often record myself reading notes and play them back while doing other stuff around the house.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying almost every day, but then something would come up and I'd fall off track. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more intensely, even if that meant pulling late nights. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the truth is I was always playing catch-up.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check on an assignment deadline or see if there were new announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to download videos or materials that I could use later. I didn't have reliable internet at home, so I had to be strategic about when and why I logged in. If a classmate mentioned something important was posted, that would also get me to check the LMS.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. Since I don't have internet at home, logging into the LMS never became a daily habit for me. I had to go to campus or find a spot with WiFi to access anything. When I was on campus, I'd try to batch everything—download what I needed, check discussions, post if I could. It was more of a planned event than a routine.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful because I could download them and listen to them multiple times offline. I'm an auditory learner, so being able to replay explanations really helped concepts stick. The discussion boards were also useful because I could see how other students understood the material, which gave me different perspectives. Those two things probably kept me afloat more than anything else.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials that were just long PDFs without any audio component. It's harder for me to stay focused when I'm just reading silently—I need to hear information to really process it. I also delayed some of the optional quizzes because they felt less urgent when I was already struggling to keep up with required work. Time was limited, so I had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would actually help me understand the material better or prepare for exams. If it had an audio component or involved some kind of interaction, I was more likely to engage with it. I also valued activities where I could see immediate feedback on whether I was getting things right. Busy work that didn't connect to the bigger picture felt like a waste.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. With required work already piling up and my limited access to the internet, optional stuff got pushed to the bottom of my list. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping them because I knew they could help, but survival mode kicked in. Required activities had consequences, so they automatically got priority.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my studying happened offline because I don't have internet at home. I'd download everything I could when I had access—videos, notes, whatever—and then study from those files for days. I also made audio recordings of key concepts and listened to them on repeat. My textbooks and printed notes were my main companions most nights.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it at all. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures, rewinding parts I didn't understand, and talking through problems out loud. I'd also study with classmates in person, working through concepts together. None of that showed up anywhere online, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download a week's worth of videos and readings, and then not log in again for days while I worked through everything offline. My login frequency probably looked terrible, but I was actually spending a lot of time with the materials. It was just happening on my laptop without any internet connection. That's the only way I could make it work with my situation.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was dealing with a lot of sadness—personal stuff that weighed on me—and my motivation just tanked. I'd stare at my notes and nothing would stick. The stress built up and I started feeling overwhelmed by how behind I was. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me get through some of that, but it was still a rough stretch.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS and just not have the mental energy to actually engage with anything. I'd scroll through, maybe download something, but my brain wasn't really there. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff—also meant I sometimes had to choose between the course and other obligations. The LMS saw me less during those times.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything because my mind was elsewhere. I was going through the motions but not actually learning. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but I'd have to go back and rewatch everything later. Stress and sadness really messed with my ability to concentrate.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was that sad, heavy feeling that made everything seem harder than it needed to be. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems. It became a cycle I struggled to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without a deadline, I'd let things slide, but once one approached, I'd kick into high gear. I'd log in more frequently near due dates, download what I needed, and work intensively. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of deadlines was often the only thing that got me to submit work. My LMS activity probably spiked right before things were due.
Q17: When I got feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. Positive comments made me feel like my effort was worth something, even when I was struggling. Critical feedback sometimes stung, but it also showed me what I needed to fix. I wish I'd gotten feedback more quickly sometimes—the delay made it hard to apply lessons to the next task.
Q18: The course structure was pretty demanding, and with eleven online courses going on, it was hard to keep track of everything. When modules were clearly organized with audio or video content, I could manage better. But when things felt scattered or text-heavy, I struggled to stay on top of it. A clearer, more consistent structure would have helped me a lot.
Q19: Having video content made engagement easier because I could listen and learn in my preferred style. What made it harder was when materials required constant internet access or when there were too many small tasks spread across the week. My lack of reliable internet meant I couldn't participate in real-time discussions or check in as often as the course expected. That structural mismatch hurt my engagement.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce work. Maybe they're dealing with personal issues, or they understand the material but freeze up when it's time to submit something. High activity might mean they're searching for help, rewatching videos, trying to grasp concepts. The gap between activity and submission could be about confidence or circumstances, not laziness.
Q21: That kind of resonates with me. My LMS activity was low because of my internet situation, but I was still putting in hours offline. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was listening to lectures, making notes, studying with peers—all invisible to the system. Performance can happen outside the platform, especially for students who learn differently or have access barriers.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you spent on certain pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you were actually thinking or just scrolling. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the conversations you have with classmates. They show the skeleton of engagement but not the soul of it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, connecting ideas to things I already know. It's showing up mentally, even when it's hard. For me, engagement is about effort and intention, whether or not that shows up in any system. It's a mindset more than a metric.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester or how hard I fought to keep going despite feeling sad and overwhelmed. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or the peer study sessions that kept me sane. It also wouldn't show that I actually care about learning—I just had barriers that made it look like I didn't. My resilience doesn't show up in login counts.
